title: extends — inherit everything
instruction:
`class Child extends Parent` gives Child all of Parent's fields and methods for free.

Run this — `AdminUser` gets `greet()` from `User` without writing it.
successMessage: admin.greet() works even though AdminUser never defined it — inherited from User. super(name, email) calls User's constructor to set up the inherited fields.

title: super() — parent constructor
instruction:
When a child class defines its own constructor, it **must** call `super(...)` as the first statement.

This ensures the parent class gets to initialise its own fields.
hint: AdminUser constructor: super(name, email); this.permissions = permissions;

codeChecks.message: Call super(name, email) first in the constructor.

successMessage: super(name, email) initialises User's name and email fields. Then this.permissions = permissions adds what AdminUser needs. If you forget super(), Java complains immediately.

title: @Override — replace the parent method
instruction:
`@Override` replaces a parent method with a new implementation.

Use `super.method()` to call the parent's version alongside your override.

`@Override` is optional but critical — it tells Java and your IDE that you mean to override.

codeChecks.message: Add @Override annotation before the overridden method.

successMessage: super.greet() calls User's version, then ' (Admin)' is appended. @Override is technically optional — but without it, a typo (grret vs greet) creates a new method instead of overriding.

title: instanceof and casting
instruction:
`instanceof` checks if an object is of a given type — including parent types.

With pattern matching (Java 16+): `if (obj instanceof AdminUser admin)` — check AND cast in one step.
successMessage: instanceof Animal is true for both Dog and Cat — they extend Animal. Pattern matching (instanceof Dog d) checks and casts in one step — Java 16+. No explicit (Dog) cast needed.

title: final and abstract classes
instruction:
- `final class` — cannot be extended (String is final!)
- `abstract class` — cannot be instantiated directly; must be extended

`abstract` methods have no body — subclasses MUST implement them.
successMessage: Abstract class + abstract methods = guaranteed implementation in subclasses. You cannot do new Shape() — it forces you to create a concrete subclass. Shape.describe() is shared without rewriting.

title: Build a vehicle hierarchy
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build this hierarchy:
- `abstract class Vehicle(String make, String model, int year)` — abstract `fuelType()` and `describe()` methods
- `class Car extends Vehicle` — fuelType() = "gasoline"
- `class ElectricCar extends Car` — fuelType() = "electric", adds `batteryRange` field
- `class Truck extends Vehicle` — fuelType() = "diesel", adds `payload` field

Create one of each. Use instanceof to identify each type. Print all their descriptions.
hint: abstract class Vehicle { abstract String fuelType(); String describe() { return make + " " + model + " (" + year + ") — " + fuelType(); } }
successMessage: A complete vehicle hierarchy — abstract base, concrete subclasses, instanceof checks. This is how real domain models are structured in enterprise Java. Chapter 11 done!
